question: What is D2DAP
answer:
# D2DAP: Drone-to-Drone Authentication Protocol

## Overview
D2DAP, which stands for "Provably Secure and Efficient Drone-to-Drone Authentication Protocol," is a robust and efficient authentication protocol designed for

Krittibas Parai , Prasanta Kumar Roy , Pankaj Kumar , SK Hafizul Islam
Abstract—Drone-to-Drone (D2D) communication has significant
applications in many real-time monitoring and surveillance
systems. However, comm

and improves system efficiency during the D2D authentication
process. The security and privacy properties of D2DAP are
validated using formal, informal, and automated tool-based
security analyses. Furthermore, its real-time performance is
evaluated on a hardware-based model across various security
levels, demonstrating remarkable results.
Index Terms—Internet-of-Drones, Drone-to-Drone communica-
tion, Security, Privacy, Physical security.

With the emergence of sophisticated technologies and new
types of attacks, existing security mechanisms for the IoD
environment may fail to ensure complete security and privacy,
thus requiring significant enhancements to bridge the gap. In
this section, four crucial security and design requirements are
identified for D2D authentication protocols.
1) System-Specific Threat Model: The security of existing
protocols is primarily focused on attack scenarios defined
by traditional threat models, such

sources.text:
an efficient D2D authentication protocol.
4) Key Storage and Security: In D2D communication, it is
desirable for any specific drone to be able to communicate
with other drones. The traditional solution is twofold. For
n drones in the network, a shared secret key must be
maintained between each pair of drones, resulting in n(n−
1)/2 keys in total, with each drone storing (n −1) keys,
which significantly increases its key storage overhead. In
the second approach, a group key is maintained for all

for D2D mutual authentication. Furthermore, D2D com-
munication does not rely on any intermediate trusted third
party for authentication or access control. Drones/nodes
can securely communicate via a direct D2D link without

protocol to ensure security against node-compromise
attacks [19], where a single key is used to authenticate
all drones in the network, but each individual drone
maintains a share of the secret key such that the key can

physical level and algorithmic level [30]. Security at the
physical level can be accomplished by using a PUF or
by storing data in tamper-proof memory. Algorithmic
level security can be achieved by implementing random
inputs and constant-time cryptographic algorithms. In
D2DAP, physical level security is achieved by integrating
a PUF module with each drone. Further, tamper-proof
memory may be integrated with each drone to mask
stored information. In the proposed D2DAP protocol, the

The Real-or-Random (RoR) model-based formal security
analysis is widely used to evaluate the advantage of an adaptive
PPT adversary A within a polynomial time bound when
performing passive or active attacks. In this model, A is allowed
to execute a polynomial number of queries defined below. The
probability of success of A is determined by the cumulative
advantage over all queries. The formal model for analyzing
the security of the proposed D2DAP protocol is defined as
follows:

Fig. 4: AVISPA-based simulation for D2DAP protocol
is executed via a secure channel encrypted using symmetric key
cryptography. The MAKA phase is triggered by Di to agree
on a session key SK with Dj. The channel characteristics
are defined by the Dolev-Yao threat model (insecure channel).
The secrecy of all identities and private keys of the drones
constitutes the secrecy goals of the protocol model. The authen-
tication goal of the model is to ensure mutual authentication
between Di and Dj. The

The overall energy consumption is evaluated and presented in
Table X and Fig. 7. It is observed that the proposed D2DAP
protocol has the lowest energy consumption at the 192-bit and
256-bit security levels. From Table X and Fig. 7, it is observed
that the energy consumption of the proposed D2DAP protocol
is higher than the protocols in [8], [27] for 128-bit security,
and the reason is that the proposed D2DAP protocol uses more
